content.full_text:
August 16, 1976


Mr. Palmer Brown Madden
Morrison & Foerster
Attorneys at Law
One Post Street
San Francisco, California 94104

Dear Mr. Madden:

This letter is to confirm my advice regarding 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 13620 as stated in your letter of July 30, 1976.

I advised that salary and reimbursement of expensespaid to an
employee for doing research and preparing data with a view to
providing support or assistance to a lobbyist is reportable by
the employer under Government Code Section 82045(b) if the
employee spends 10 percent or more of his compensated time in
any one month providing this type of assistance or engaging in
other activities described in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 13620.
Moreover, payments to an employee who spends 10 percent or more
of his compensated time during a month developing material upon
which direct communication with legislative or administrative
officials is based, or which is transmitted, are reportable
under Government Code Section 82045(d) and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sec-
tion 13620(a)(4). On the other hand, if the employee's time is
spent gathering information for use by the general committee in
its deliberations, and not for the support or assistance of the
lobbyist or as the basis for direct communication with officials,
payments to the employee are not reportable payments to influence
legislative or administrative action.

I hope this information is of assistance.

Sincerely,


Nan Hambleton
Special Compliance Representative

MORRISON & FOERSTER
ONE POST STREET
SAN FRANCISCO 94104
(415) 566-1310
TELEX 34-0184 CABLE MOFO

523 WEST SIXTH STREET
LOS ANGELES 90014
(213) 626-9900

July 30, 1976

Ms. Nan Hambleton
Fair Political
Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
1100 "K" Street Building
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Nan:

This is to confirm our conversations of Tuesday,
July 27, 1976. I put the following fact pattern to you and
asked whether the acts set forth would trigger any reporting
requirement under the 1974 Fair Political Practices Act.

On numerous occasions, clients of our firm, such
as Crocker National Bank, have employees that participate in
trade associations such as the Chamber of Commerce or the
California Bankers Association, or the like. Sometimes
these employees, during the course of their employment, are
involved in various committees that review proposed legisla-
tion. Typically, such an employee is a member of one or two
type of committees. These committees are typical of those
found in many trade associations.

The first type of committee is a Subcommittee that
reviews numerous pieces of proposed legislation and selects
certain pieces that are of interest to the subcommittee.
These individual pieces of legislation are given further
review to determine the impact of the legislation on the
interests of the trade association and a recommendation is
made to a Central Committee.

The Central Committee is the second type of committee
that is also typically found in trade associations and the
like. The Central Committee receives the recommendations of
the subcommittee along with a statement of arguments, pro
and con, and makes a final decision as to whether to endorse,
oppose or stand neutral on the legislation. The Central
Committee, after due deliberation, makes a final decision
and informs a designated advocate (who may or may not be a
lobbyist) of the association's views with an eye towards

Ms. Nan Hambleton
July 30, 1976
Page 2.

such advocate undertaking to inform members of the California
Legislature or members of California State Agencies of the
position of the association. The association subsequently
monitors the work of the advocate.

You informed me that, with the above fact pattern,
the employee would not trigger any reporting obligation
since, in your view, the activities outlined are too remote.
You cautioned me that reporting obligations would be triggered
if the employee were to become further involved by engaging
in activities to support the advocate's effort, such as
undertaking, after the group has taken a position, to prepare
statistics for the advocate.

I would appreciate it if you would be kind enough
to confirm for me that this letter has fairly stated the
substance of our conversation.

Very truly yours,

Palmer Brown Madden
for
MORRISON & FOERSTER

PBM:jck

sections.question: whether the acts set forth would trigger any reporting requirement under the 1974 Fair Political Practices Act
sections.question_synthetic: Do employees of law firm clients who participate in trade association committees that review proposed legislation and make recommendations to advocacy efforts trigger reporting obligations under the Fair Political Practices Act?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a law firm to FPPC staff, not an FPPC response letter. The document contains no formal CONCLUSION section; instead it references an oral conversation and requests confirmation. The document is heavily degraded with OCR errors and missing text throughout. The question_synthetic is inferred from the stated fact pattern and oral advice referenced.

embedding.summary: A Morrison & Foerster attorney requests confirmation of a prior conversation with FPPC staff regarding whether employees participating in trade association legislative review committees trigger reporting obligations, noting that FPPC staff advised no reporting is required for the described activities unless employees become more directly involved in supporting advocacy efforts.
